triadic_ontology.findings: The concept of triadic ontology and relational metaphysics has been explored in various papers, including 'Reality Is Triune: A Metaphysical Argument for Agápe as First Principle' by Mark Pelley and 'Relational Ontologies and Trinitarian Metaphysics: Subsistences, Events and Gunk' by Damiano Migliorini. These papers argue that reality cannot be grounded in solitary being, neutral structure, or dyadic relation, and that a triune structure is necessary to sustain distinction, unity, and generativity without contradiction. Additionally, the paper 'Triadic Relational Ontology: From Primordial Separation to Relational Field' by Christopher Isabelle develops Triadic Relational Ontology as a continuation of Primordial Separation Ontology, addressing the conditions under which intelligibility arises and is sustained across separation. The Trinitarian Standard Model, presented by Coherent Intelligence, proposes a formal isomorphism between the mathematical structure of the Standard Model's gaug [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: Recent advances in multi-agent AI research systems have led to the development of autonomous pipelines for scientific research. The Mimosa framework, for example, is an evolving multi-agent framework that automatically synthesizes task-specific multi-agent workflows and iteratively refines them through experimental feedback. Another example is the CORAL infrastructure, which is a robust, lightweight infrastructure for building organizations of autonomous AI agents that run experiments, share knowledge, and continuously improve solutions. In terms of reasoning models, prompt engineering has become a crucial aspect of controlling effort, not technique. The 2026 playbook for prompting reasoning models includes using Claude's adaptive thinking budget, GPT-5.4's reasoning_effort parameter, and Gemini 2.5 Deep Think's tool-use chains. Old tricks like chain-of-thought now hurt performance on these models, and it's recommended to let them reason internally and prompt for outcomes, not steps.

uap.findings: The Pentagon has released a second wave of declassified UFO files, adding new documents and fresh videos that capture unidentified objects appearing across multiple regions of the world. The disclosure is part of a broader transparency initiative launched under President Donald Trump, with officials saying the public can now review the materials and draw their own conclusions. Despite renewed global fascination surrounding extraterrestrial theories, Pentagon officials maintain there is still no confirmed evidence linking the sightings to alien life. A UFO insider claims the US has bodies of 4 different alien species from downed spacecraft in government custody. The Pentagon released more declassified UFO files on Friday as part of a transparency effort ordered by President Donald Trump. Newly declassified UFO files reveal unexplained encounters. classroom_ready.title: Token Boards in the High School Classroom
classroom_ready.summary: Token boards are a simple but powerful tool for promoting self-regulation and motivation in high school classrooms. They can be tailored to individual students' needs and interests, and can help teachers encourage independence and responsibility.

classroom_ready.title: Lesson 1: Designing an Effective Token Economy System
classroom_ready.summary: This lesson provides a comprehensive checklist for implementing a token economy system in the classroom. It covers the basics of token economies, including the importance of individualization and the need for clear expectations and transparency.

classroom_ready.title: Classroom Token System: 8 ABA Steps to Reinforce Appropriate Behavior
classroom_ready.summary: This article provides a step-by-step guide to implementing a token economy system in the classroom, including choosing and defining target behaviors, setting up the token system, and fading support over time.

pedagogy_pieces.title: Truth, Storytelling, and Identity in Everything Sad Is Untrue
pedagogy_pieces.summary: This lesson plan explores the themes of truth, storytelling, and identity in the novel Everything Sad Is Untrue. It provides a close-reading activity and discussion questions to help students delve deeply into the text and explore the complexities of the protagonist's experiences.

english_lesson_plans.notes: The search results provided a range of resources for high school English language arts lesson plans, classroom management strategies, and digital token economy systems. The top results included articles and lesson plans from reputable sources such as Nicolette Lesniak, KNILT, and Facing History and Ourselves. The results also included a range of topics, including token boards, token economy systems, and close reading activities. Overall, the search results provided a wealth of information and resources for teachers looking to improve their classroom management and instructional strategies.

newyorker.title: The Useless Beauty of Christo and Jeanne-Claude
newyorker.dek: Plans for a forgotten art work by the late duo were found in an airshaft. Now, with the help of Christo’s nephew, “Air Package on a Ceiling” is finally on view, at Gagosian. Anna Russell writes.
newyorker.text:
In 1964, the married artists Christo and Jeanne-Claude moved out of the Chelsea Hotel and into the top two floors of a loft on Howard Street, in SoHo. Sure, they were broke—the hotel proprietor said they could pay him back later—but they were jazzed about their new digs. The place had big windows and an airshaft. (Later, they bought the building; it was the seventies.) Eventually, the airshaft became a storage unit, and it was there, in 2018, that Christo’s studio manager discovered a scale model, complete with electrical wiring and preparatory drawings, for a project the artist had forgotten about from half a century earlier. Eureka!
The other day, at the Gagosian in London, several art installers were hoisting ropes and clambering up ladders, in order to mount the work in a gallery for the first time. The piece, “Air Package on a Ceiling,” was designed in 1968 for the Institute of Contemporary Art, in Philadelphia, and involves a giant sheet of plastic suspended from the ceiling and crossed by ropes that make it bulge and fold. (It never reached the I.C.A., owing to technical concerns.) The effect is disorienting, as if the air had turned solid. Overseeing things was Christo’s nephew, Vladimir Yavachev, who was dressed in layers of black. “I mean, it’s up there—it’s not falling,” he said, peering at the plastic.
Yavachev began working with Christo in 1990, when he was a teen-ager, and newly freed from the constraints of Communist Bulgaria. He arrived in New York having never met his uncle, who had escaped the Eastern Bloc years earlier, by bribing a customs officer, and had never returned. “In Communist Bulgaria, there was a word for it,” Yavachev said. “You don’t become an immigrant, you become a nevuzvrashtenetz, which means ‘the one that can never come back.’ ” Christo and Jeanne-Claude travelled the world wrapping things: cars, islands, the Sydney coastline. “The enthusiasm was incredible,” Yavachev said.
Christo died in 2020—Jeanne-Claude in 2009—but the duo left detailed instructions for the posthumous installation of “Air Package” and other works, including “L’Arc de Triomphe, Wrapped,” which Yavachev completed in 2021. At eighty, Christo was involved in the final planning. By the time the installation began, his presence was greatly missed. “He had always been like a jackrabbit running around to see a project from every point of view that you can,” Yavachev said. “The way he would look at them, it was like a little kid looking at a cake, like you want to eat it. It was always like this, always ‘Look, look, look, look!’ ”
In the gallery, a man in a ball cap had climbed a ladder to examine the folds of the plastic sheet. “More slack,” someone said. “More folds.” Yavachev had flown in that morning from Abu Dhabi, where he has been working on realizing the duo’s most ambitious work, “The Mastaba,” a tomblike structure made of oil barrels in the desert, which, if completed, would be one of the world’s largest contemporary sculptures by volume. “Inshallah,” Yavachev said. He has been working on it for twenty years, with no end date in sight.
“Jeanne-Claude always used to say we do these projects for ourselves, and if other people enjoy it, that’s a bonus,” he went on. Many of the works were dreamed up decades before they became reality. “The projects find their time,” he said. He recalled a phrase he had learned from Bono, on the roof of the Arc de Triomphe. “Cathedral thinking,” he said. “Cathedral thinking is when you start something that you know that you cannot finish. Any architect of a cathedral, it will take two hundred years to finish a cathedral! You’re never going to see it. It’s very similar.”
In their lifetimes, the artists scrupulously avoided assigning meaning to their art works. “Christo always said, ‘I did not invent the politics in the Reichstag,’ ” Yavachev said. (They once wrapped the Reichstag.) “Coming from a totalitarian country, he never wanted to use art as propaganda. It was always art for art’s sake, just as a visual medium without any message. Though the message is there and anyone’s interpretation is legitimate.” “L’Arc de Triomphe, Wrapped” became a symbol of post-Covid unity, even though the piece was envisioned back in 1961.
“The power of this, and why it’s so strong, is because it’s an art work and because it’s useless,” Yavachev said. “Nobody needs it. This uselessness of stuff is the thing that makes us human.” What did he think visitors to the desert would make of “The Mastaba” in five, ten, or twenty years, once it was complete? “Oh, no, no,” he said. “I don’t like to speculate.” ♦

literary_pick.title: Orbital
literary_pick.author: Samantha Harvey
literary_pick.summary: Orbital is a novel about six astronauts rotating in the International Space Station, exploring the fragility of human life and the effects of being in space on their relationships and sense of identity.
